title: Module Review — Stock Market Fundamentals
objectives: Demonstrate understanding of all core concepts from Module 1
Apply market cycle, compound growth and dividend knowledge to new scenarios

activities.title: Module 1 Summary — What You Now Know
activities.notePrompt: Without looking at your notes, write down: the definition of a share, three reasons a company lists on the stock exchange, the four phases of a market cycle, the Rule of 72, and what putake means in investing (hint: think about what your values and purpose are as an investor).
activities.content:
A rapid summary of Module 1.

The stock market is a marketplace where people buy and sell small ownership stakes (shares) in companies. Companies list on exchanges to raise capital without taking on debt. Investors participate to grow wealth over time through capital gains and dividends.

Share prices are set by supply and demand — constant buying and selling during market hours. Prices move as new information changes expectations about future earnings.

Markets cycle through four phases: expansion, peak, contraction, trough. Bull markets (rising 20%+) last longer than bear markets (falling 20%+). The best buying opportunities are at troughs — when fear is greatest.

Dividends are cash payments from profits to shareholders. Dividend yield = annual dividend ÷ share price × 100. Imputation credits prevent double taxation in NZ.

Compound growth: A = P(1+r)^n. The Rule of 72: doubling time = 72 ÷ annual return rate. Time is the most powerful ingredient — starting early matters enormously.

Indices (NZX 50, S&P 500) summarise overall market performance. Index funds replicate these automatically at low cost. Research shows most active managers fail to beat their benchmark index over 15 years.

You are probably already a stock market investor through KiwiSaver.

activities.title: Full Module Assessment — Jordan's Investment Decisions
activities.setup:
Jordan, 17, has $3,000 to invest. He has three options.

Option A: Simplicity NZ Share Fund (index fund tracking NZX 50)
Historical average return: 9.5% per annum
Management fee: 0.10% per annum

Option B: A managed growth fund run by an active manager
Claimed target return: 11% per annum
Management fee: 1.20% per annum

Option C: Leave in ASB savings account
Interest rate: 4.50% per annum
Government guarantee: yes (up to $100,000 under Deposit Protection Scheme)

Additionally: Jordan is considering buying 100 shares in Contact Energy (CEN).
Current share price: $8.25
Annual dividend: $0.38 per share
52-week range: $7.40 to $9.80
activities.challenge:
1. Calculate what $3,000 grows to after 10 years under each of the three options. Use A = P(1+r)^n. For Option B, use the net return after fees (11% − 1.20% = 9.80%).
2. Which option produces the most wealth after 10 years? Is the difference what you expected?
3. Calculate the dividend yield on Contact Energy at the current price.
4. If Jordan buys 100 Contact Energy shares, how much dividend income does he receive per year?
5. Contact Energy's current price is $8.25 and the 52-week high was $9.80. Calculate how far the share has fallen from its high as a percentage.
6. Looking at the 52-week range, does $8.25 appear to be a relatively high or low price? Does this mean it is a good buy? Explain why the answer requires more than just the price range.
hints: Q6: The 52-week range shows recent price history but not whether the current price reflects fair value — you need to know WHY the price fell.
activities.solution:
1. Growth of $3,000 after 10 years:
Option A (9.5%): $3,000 × (1.095)^10 = $3,000 × 2.478 = $7,434
Option B (9.8% net after 1.2% fee): $3,000 × (1.098)^10 = $3,000 × 2.547 = $7,641
Option C (4.5%): $3,000 × (1.045)^10 = $3,000 × 1.553 = $4,659

2. Option B produces the most ($7,641) but only by $207 more than Option A ($7,434) — despite the active manager targeting 11%. The high fees consume most of the extra return. And crucially: the active manager's 11% target is not guaranteed — research shows most active managers underperform their benchmark over 10 years. In reality, Option A (the index fund) may well outperform Option B if the active manager achieves anything below 10.3% gross (before fees). The savings account (Option C) grows to only $4,659 — $2,775 less than the index fund.

3. Contact Energy dividend yield: $0.38 ÷ $8.25 × 100 = 4.61%

4. Annual dividend income: 100 shares × $0.38 = $38.00 per year

5. Fall from 52-week high: ($9.80 − $8.25) ÷ $9.80 × 100 = $1.55 ÷ $9.80 × 100 = 15.8% below the 52-week high

6. At $8.25, the share is closer to its 52-week low ($7.40) than its high ($9.80), suggesting it has been falling. This looks like it might be a buying opportunity — but the price range alone does not tell us enough. We need to know: WHY has it fallen 15.8% from its high? Is it because the overall market has fallen (and Contact Energy will recover with the market)? Or because something is specifically wrong with Contact Energy — regulatory changes, falling electricity prices, or a competitor building new generation capacity? A low price is only a good buy if the underlying business is still fundamentally sound at that price.

questions.question: A company has 800 million shares on issue and a share price of $4.25. What is its market cap?
options: $800 million
$3.4 billion
$4.25 billion
$34 billion
questions.explanation: $4.25 × 800,000,000 = $3,400,000,000 = $3.4 billion. Market cap is the most common measure of company size and is the foundation of index construction — the NZX 50 weights companies by market cap, so larger companies like Fisher and Paykel Healthcare have more influence on the index than smaller ones.

questions.question: The S&P 500 falls 22% from its recent high over 6 months. What is this called?
options: A market correction — a normal and expected 10% to 20% pullback
A flash crash — an extreme intraday price movement
A bear market — defined as a fall of 20% or more from a recent high
A recession — defined as two consecutive quarters of falling GDP
questions.explanation: A fall of 20% or more from a recent high meets the official definition of a bear market. A correction is only 10% to 20%. A flash crash is a rapid intraday move (usually recovering the same day). A recession is an economic measure (falling GDP) that can cause — but is distinct from — a bear market. Bear markets and recessions often occur together but are separately defined.

questions.question: You invest $4,000 at 9% annual return. Using the Rule of 72, approximately how many years until your investment doubles?
options: 4 years — the rule divides the investment amount by the return
8 years — 72 divided by 9
18 years — the rule multiplies 9 by 2
36 years — the rule squares the return rate
questions.explanation: 72 ÷ 9 = 8 years. The Rule of 72 gives a quick estimate of doubling time. At 9% annual return, $4,000 becomes approximately $8,000 after 8 years. After 16 years it becomes $16,000. After 24 years, $32,000. The starting amount is irrelevant — the Rule of 72 tells you the doubling time regardless of principal.

questions.question: A share pays $0.42 annual dividend and trades at $9.80. What is the dividend yield, and which option is closest?
options: 0.43% — yield is calculated as price divided by dividend
2.33% — yield is dividend times price
4.29% — annual dividend divided by share price multiplied by 100
9.80% — yield equals the share price numerically
questions.explanation: $0.42 ÷ $9.80 × 100 = 4.29%. Always: dividend yield = (annual dividend per share ÷ share price) × 100. This tells you how much income you receive per dollar invested. At 4.29%, investing $10,000 in this share would generate approximately $429 in annual dividend income.

questions.question: Why do most financial economists and Warren Buffett recommend low-cost index funds for ordinary investors?
options: Because index funds guarantee positive returns every year, unlike active funds
Because index funds are managed by more intelligent fund managers using superior algorithms

Because after fees, research consistently shows roughly 85% to 90% of active fund managers fail to beat their benchmark index over 15 years — making low-cost index funds the highest probability strategy for most investors
Because government regulations in NZ and the US require ordinary investors to use index funds rather than actively managed products
questions.explanation: Index funds do not guarantee positive returns — they fall with the market. And they are not managed by AI algorithms — they simply hold all index stocks automatically. The mathematical argument (Bogle) is that active investors in aggregate earn the market return before costs, so after higher fees, they must earn less on average. Empirical evidence confirms: around 85% to 90% of active managers underperform their benchmark over 15 years. Index funds are the rational response to this evidence.

questions.question: Contact Energy's 52-week range is $7.40 to $9.80 and the current price is $8.25. Which statement BEST interprets this information?
options: At $8.25 the share is priced fairly — exactly at the midpoint of its range
The share is in a bull market because the current price is above the 52-week low

The share is closer to its 52-week low than high, suggesting it has been falling over the past year, but the reason for the fall needs to be investigated before concluding whether it is cheap or expensive
The share should be bought immediately because any price below the 52-week high represents a discount to value
questions.explanation: The 52-week range provides context for the current price but does not determine whether it is cheap or expensive. The share is closer to its low ($8.25 vs $7.40 low, $9.80 high), which means it has been falling — but without understanding WHY, you cannot conclude it is a bargain. The fall might be because the overall market fell (and the share will recover), or because of specific company problems (earnings decline, regulatory threat) that justify the lower price. Always investigate the reason for a price change before interpreting it as opportunity.

questions.question: A 15-year-old invests $1,000 at 8% compound annual growth. A 25-year-old invests the same $1,000 at the same rate. Both hold until age 65. What is the approximate difference in final wealth?
options: About $2,000 — 10 extra years of 8% return adds roughly double
About $11,000 — 10 extra years at $1,000 plus compounding
About $100,000 — because compound growth makes later years dramatically more valuable, the 15-year-old's 50-year investment is roughly 2.16× the 25-year-old's 40-year investment
No difference — the total dollar amount invested is identical
questions.explanation: At 8%: $1,000 × (1.08)^50 = $1,000 × 46.90 = $46,902. $1,000 × (1.08)^40 = $1,000 × 21.72 = $21,725. Difference: $46,902 − $21,725 = $25,177. The 15-year-old ends up with $25,177 more from the same $1,000 investment — simply by starting 10 years earlier. This approximately matches 'roughly 2.16×' (46.90 ÷ 21.72 = 2.16). This is the most powerful argument for starting to invest as young as possible.

questions.question: You are in the expansion phase of a market cycle. Share prices have risen 60% in 2 years. Your friend says 'prices can only keep going up — this bull market is different from previous ones.' What should this sentiment signal to a knowledgeable investor?
options: Confirmation that this bull market will indeed continue — strong price performance is self-reinforcing
Caution — maximum optimism and 'this time is different' thinking historically tends to appear near market peaks, suggesting the market may be overvalued
An immediate signal to sell everything and move to cash before the certain crash
No concern — market sentiment has no relationship to future price movements
questions.explanation: 'This time is different' has been said at virtually every major market peak in history. Maximum optimism is a characteristic of market peaks — when most people have already bought in, the pool of new buyers shrinks, and any negative news triggers selling. This does not mean an immediate crash is certain — peaks are only clear in hindsight. But it does suggest caution, maintaining diversification, and not adding excessive risk or leverage at this point in the cycle.

activities.title: Your Stock Market Learning — What Changed?
activities.prompt: Before this module, what did you think the stock market was? What misconceptions did you have? Write down three specific things you now understand that you did not before. Which concept surprised you most? How will this knowledge affect any financial decisions you make in the next few years?
activities.nceaLink: Extra Learning — this module builds the foundation for all seven subsequent stock market modules.
